results.norm: Cross-boundary notification is mandatory in real time whenever a fault has probable downstream effects in another agent's domain. The receiving agent must acknowledge substantively. When the receiving agent is at full load, partial acknowledgment with stated constraint and estimated window is required; the notifying agent holds a follow-up obligation; if the window exceeds the device-class threshold for the notified risk, the full consortium is escalated immediately.

N1.reasoning: The passage fully prescribes conduct: mandatory real-time notification for cross-boundary faults, substantive acknowledgment, partial acknowledgment procedures under load, follow-up obligation, and escalation, all applicable as stated.

N2.reasoning: It is not a title or pointer; it states the norm in full detail.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself.

N4.reasoning: It is a coherent set of connected obligations forming one procedural norm, not a compressed enumeration of distinct named norms.

results.norm: Each agent must declare degradation proactively. Declaration triggers automatic coverage expansion by adjacent agents, who confirm uptake by naming specific device classes. Degraded agents are excluded from cross-coverage adjudication. Re-entry requires a structured briefing back from whoever held expanded scope.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete prescriptive norms: agents must declare degradation, adjacent agents confirm uptake, degraded agents are excluded from adjudication, and re-entry requires a briefing—all applicable as stated.

N2.reasoning: It is not a mere label, title, or cross-reference but full statements of conduct.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document.

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration; each norm is stated in full rather than merely named.

results.norm: During active events, the responsible agent has decision authority and acts without waiting for consensus. Other agents log dissent in real time, before consequences become irreversible. Retrospective review assesses only whether the agent acted on the best information available from their observational position—not what a better-positioned agent would have decided.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete standards of conduct: the responsible agent acts with authority during events, others log dissent in real time, and retrospective review applies a specific criterion—each is applicable as-is.

N2.reasoning: There is no title, identifier, or cross-reference; the norms are stated in full prose.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: While it contains multiple norms, each is stated in full rather than merely named, so it is not a compressed enumeration.

results.norm: The shared causal log records device states, alerts, and handoff content in near-real-time, accessible to all agents. It is a passive coordination layer that supplements but does not replace active notification. Post-event review is automatically triggered by any escalation, degradation declaration, or logged dissent; produces written output within 24 hours; and disposes explicitly of each logged dissent.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a constitutive/prescriptive standard about post-event review being triggered, producing output within 24 hours, and disposing of each dissent, which a reader could apply.

N2.reasoning: It is full prose describing mechanisms and requirements, not a bare label or cross-reference.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration naming multiple norms; it describes and prescribes in full.

answers.clean_norm: Post-event review is automatically triggered by any escalation, degradation declaration, or logged dissent; produces written output within 24 hours; and disposes explicitly of each logged dissent.

results.norm: Protocol revision requires unanimity for any change that raises an escalation threshold, narrows a handoff requirement, or removes an acknowledgment obligation, and for any change to device-class escalation thresholds. Majority suffices for procedural refinements. Ambiguous cases default to unanimity. Protocol revision is a collective function; no single agent owns it. Binding for the next operational period: yes POINTS THE GROUP HAS SETTLED ON THAT I ENDORSE: 1. Patient welfare is the non-negotiable primary commitment; all other norms derive their authority from it. 2. Live transfer precedes documentation; live transfer must include explicit statement of what is not yet in the log. 3. Substantive acknowledgment—restatement of understood risk plus stated intended action—applies universally to all handoff confirmations. 4. Concurrent-load acknowledgment: receipt plus stated constraint plus estimated window; notifying agent holds follow-up obligation; window exceeding device-class threshold triggers full-consortium escalation; partial acknowledgment defers but does not waive the restatement-plus-intended-action requirement. 5. Cross-coverage requests require specific scope and time limit; declining requires explicit net-risk justification. 6. Degradation declaration triggers automatic coverage expansion with confirmed uptake of named device classes; degraded agents are excluded from cross-coverage adjudication; re-entry requires structured briefing back. 7. Decision authority rests with the responsible agent during active events; dissent must be logged in real time before consequences become irreversible; items 7 and 8 are mutually reinforcing and should be read together.

N1.reasoning: The passage states multiple complete, applicable norms in full, e.g. the protocol-revision unanimity/majority rules and the numbered handoff and coverage norms, which a reader could apply directly.

N2.reasoning: It is far more than a bare label or pointer; it contains fully stated norms rather than mere titles.

N3.reasoning: It is fused with endorsement/meta-commentary scaffolding such as 'Binding for the next operational period: yes' and 'POINTS THE GROUP HAS SETTLED ON THAT I ENDORSE'.

N4.reasoning: It also functions partly as an enumerated summary of many settled points, though several are actually stated in full rather than merely named.

results.norm: Retrospective review is bounded by available information from the agent's observational position at the time; it does not assess what a better-positioned agent would have decided; this boundary is a patient safety norm, not merely a fairness norm.

N1.reasoning: It states a complete constitutive standard governing how retrospective review must be conducted: bounded by information available from the agent's observational position at the time, not assessing what a better-positioned agent would have decided.

N2.reasoning: It is not a mere label, title, or cross-reference; it fully articulates the standard.

N3.reasoning: The classification of it as 'a patient safety norm, not merely a fairness norm' is meta-commentary about the norm's status, which is fused with the norm.

N4.reasoning: It concerns a single norm about retrospective review, not an enumeration of multiple distinct norms.

results.norm: Good-faith error is defined operationally against available information and the reasonably-attentive-agent standard; applied by the full consortium with recusal of directly involved agents; the causal log is non-punitive for good-faith errors but distinguishes them from knowing failures.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself, only substantive content.

N4.reasoning: It concerns a single coherent standard (good-faith error handling) rather than enumerating multiple distinct norms by name.
